title: A Little Community Called Rolling Wall
summary: Interviewed by John Dittmer with videographer John Bishop in Durant on March 11, 2013, Walter Bruce, a leader of the Holmes County Movement, recalls being born and raised in the country community of Rolling Wall to parents who sharecropped a few acres, and moving to Durant in the early 1960s.

title: A Baseball League and a Gospel Group
summary: Bruce describes running a community baseball little league for fifteen or sixteen years and starting a gospel group, finishing the eighth grade at the Rolling Wall school and the twelfth grade at Ambrose High in Lexington, being the youngest of thirteen children (six brothers and seven sisters), and the family's Methodist church reached by walking and crossing a creek on a felled tree.

title: He Couldn't Give Me the Dollar
topic: The Land and a Boyhood Standoff
summary: Bruce recounts the family farming two or three acres they did not own and his learning to plow behind the mule, and a boyhood confrontation in which a white man kept an animal that Bruce believed was owed to him, refused to release it, and Bruce went and took it himself, an early instance of standing his ground.

title: I Never Hated White for Right
summary: Bruce reflects on living under Jim Crow segregation in a county that was mostly black, the all-white control and law enforcement, his limited contact with whites while growing up, and his refusal to hate white people even when he disliked how they acted.

part: A Carpenter and a Gospel Singer
title: A Great Gift From God
summary: Bruce explains that he became a carpenter from about his sixteenth birthday without ever going to school for it, calling it a gift from God, working with two friends as a crew and building houses for both white and black families across the county, homes he says are all still standing.

title: Fifty-Four Years of Singing
topic: The Gospel Group and Movement Music
summary: Bruce describes the gospel group he kept for fifty-four years, singing at homes and across Tallahatchie and Leflore counties and as far as Chicago, doing radio broadcasts and making recordings, outliving the other original members, and reflects on why gospel singing mattered so much in the mass meetings of the movement.

part: What Good Is a Job Without Freedom
title: What Good Is a Job If You Ain't Got No Freedom
summary: Bruce recalls weighing whether taking part in the civil rights movement would cost him the building jobs he depended on as a contractor, deciding that a job without freedom was worthless, and how, going out to meetings at night across the county, he credited God that he was never harmed.

title: Not Too Far From Here
topic: Emmett Till and the Early Network
summary: Bruce remembers the community's reaction to the lynching of Emmett Till near Greenwood about fifty or sixty miles away, his sense that Till may have been involved in civil rights, and how he later came to know Medgar Evers and the NAACP and Aaron Henry, seeing in the Delta how white people treated black people and getting ticketed on Highway 12 to discourage him from the meetings, where SNCC was very active.

title: Registered in the Basement of the Post Office
summary: Bruce recounts the 1964 Freedom Summer registration effort, when black residents first registered in the basement of the post office before being allowed into the courthouse, the literacy questions used to fail people (counting strands of hair or bubbles in a bucket of water), and the volunteers who stayed with black families and were not afraid.

title: The First Fourteen to the Courthouse
summary: Bruce describes the Meredith March Against Fear and the Freedom Democratic Party spreading across sixteen or seventeen communities, then the first fourteen Holmes County black residents who went to the courthouse to register in 1963, among them Hartman Turnbow, whose powerful voice he recalls, and the independent Mileston landowners who, owning their own farms, could not be evicted; he notes the firebombing of Turnbow's house, Bob Moses, and Alma Mitchell Carnegie.

title: They Found Him Dead
summary: Bruce tells of people from Holmes County jailed at the fairgrounds during the Jackson demonstrations, and of J.J. Young, a ballplayer he knew not to drink who was locked up as if drunk, had a bad heart, was refused his medicine, and was found dead, after which Bruce insisted on viewing the body, found no scars, and the community decided to begin a boycott.

title: We All Going to Stay in There Together
summary: Bruce recounts being arrested in Lexington over a march, accused of beating a drum he insisted was his to play, and locked up with about twelve people; he refused to be bailed out alone so the group would leave together, then describes a second march that so filled the square the police could not move their car, the demonstrators winning what they asked before calling it off, and his being treated decently in his only arrest.

title: Still President of the Freedom Democratic Party
summary: Bruce explains how the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, formed to challenge the all-white delegation at the national convention in Atlantic City, elected him president, a post he still held at the time of the interview, and made him coordinator over seventeen or eighteen communities; he describes the precinct and county-wide meetings that chose delegates and the January 1965 congressional challenge to seat Fannie Lou Hamer, Annie Devine, and Victoria Gray.

title: You Can't Read a Blueprint
summary: Bruce discusses why most preachers and teachers stayed out of the movement early, the teachers fearing white superintendents and the preachers wary of bank loans, with some ministers trading favors with whites and passing information back; he then tells how, after a teacher was fired, he answered the teachers who doubted him by saying they could not read a blueprint and threatened a boycott that got the teacher rehired, his lesson that divided people are kept powerless.

title: The Clinic Started Right Here
topic: The Lorenzis, the Center, and Health Care
summary: Bruce recalls the white volunteers Sue and Henry Lorenzi, who came in the fall of 1964 and stayed five years, and the volunteer-built community center that he and Henry spearheaded, where a health clinic began in 1964; he contrasts the earlier days when black families could not afford a hospital and relied on a white country doctor's house calls and segregated waiting rooms with the community health centers now serving Lexington and Durant, part of a national movement he says started there.

title: Two Black Members on the Board
topic: The School Board and the Cafe
summary: Bruce describes his political organizing in Durant beginning with the segregated city school, where the mayor, a lawyer, helped get him and another black man appointed to the school board, and the failed push to merge with the county school that whites fought until a separate school was made and many white families moved out of town; he then tells of integrating the town cafe, where black residents were afraid until a few went in and met no further trouble.

title: Take Down That Sign
topic: The Ice Cream Stand and the Dog
summary: Bruce recounts integrating the ice cream stand that kept separate black and white water fountains and signs, where a man tied up a German police dog until Bruce warned the mayor to have it moved, and how he forced the colored and white signs taken down, after which black residents could go in either side, part of reclaiming the square where his group also sang.

title: Keep On Hauling and Haul Them All Back
summary: Bruce tells how a grocery store fired a black worker after a fight with a white worker, prompting a Wednesday-night decision to boycott; several white families honored it, saying they could shop elsewhere, and a driver hauling a load of chickens turned back rather than unload, telling the owner to keep hauling them all back, and the store eventually went out of business.

title: Nothing But Blue Lights
summary: Bruce recounts being called to a bad incident at a school in a neighboring county after desegregation began, driving over without taking a gun so he could not be jailed, finding the place surrounded by blue lights and gathered white people, and walking in to ask for the principal as a man pressed him on whether he was FBI.

title: They Were Going Together
summary: Bruce describes finding the black students too afraid to talk and tracking down the story behind the arrest of a young man: that he and a girl had been seeing each other, that she went home with him after her parents found out, and that he was jailed on a rape accusation, a charge later dropped when the girl could not be produced for the court date.

part: Return Fire on a Country Road
title: They Named Them Christian Schools
summary: Bruce recalls the 1969 U.S. Supreme Court Holmes County decision ending all deliberate speed and ordering immediate integration, and how some white parents enrolled their children while many withdrew to a private academy in West that the citizens' council started and renamed a Christian school, leaving the public schools largely black with black teachers and a black superintendent.
related_events: Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education

title: Whatever I Said, Someone Would Try It
summary: Bruce reflects on Sue Lorenzi Sojourner's account that his Durant meetings drew more people, money, and participation than anywhere in Holmes County, and on how he moved people past the fear of losing their jobs with patience and persistence, describing the country shotgun house where they met every Wednesday night and the carloads of white people who shot up the top of the building.

title: Shoot All the Building
summary: Bruce tells how he organized three or four armed men on each side of the meeting house to return fire the next Wednesday when the carloads came through, the whites fleeing as if they had met machine guns, and how, after moving the meetings to a country church, SNCC members arriving spotted a gas-soaked rope the night riders had readied to burn the church down.

title: We Going to Be Calling You for the Ambulance
summary: Bruce recounts phoning the sheriff he calls a redneck, who would not act, and warning him that the next call would be for an ambulance because they would kill anyone who came, after which a deputy was sent; he explains that he himself never carried a gun, knowing he would be framed if caught with one, and only meant to call an ambulance the night he thought a boy had been killed.

title: What Good Is Freedom Without It
topic: Self-Defense and the Voting Rights Act
summary: Bruce describes keeping armed people at the country meetings to make sure no one stopped them from gathering, and how the 1965 Voting Rights Act, with federal registrars, changed the movement and finally drew in the teachers and preachers who had been too afraid before, the teachers reassured once black students were attending.

part: The 1967 Election and a Last Word
title: Robert Clark Goes to the Legislature
summary: Bruce explains how candidates for the 1967 elections were chosen at the third-Sunday county-wide meeting, following Ralph Hayes's 1966 run for Congress, with Robert Clark becoming the first African American elected to the Mississippi state legislature in the twentieth century; he describes vetting candidates by their support of the community meetings and the decision to run an independent slate to the November election rather than the Democratic primary, judging the odds better.

title: They Knew We Were Together
summary: Bruce describes working as coordinator on election day, driving community to community across the county where every place had a meeting and poll watchers, and how intimidation at the polls stayed limited because whites knew the black community was organized and unafraid, with Holmes County the strongest of its kind while neighboring Madison County still saw intimidation.

title: I Never Put No Money in My Pocket
summary: Bruce closes on Holmes County remaining the strongest movement county with the Freedom Democratic Party still alive, the support of his wife through years of constant night meetings, and his insistence that he never hated white people but only how they treated black people, was always outspoken, and never took money for himself.

title: It Should Be Mixed
summary: Bruce gives his final reflection that being the majority does not make a thing right, that he never believed in an all-black or all-white arrangement but always in a mixed one, and thanks the interviewers as the recording closes with the Library of Congress and Smithsonian presentation tag.
